coredata.dc:title: Bacterial cellulose production from wastewater and the influence of its porosity on the fluorescence intensity of prepared carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Huanyu
Wang, Jing
Yin, Heng
Wang, Chuandong
Zhang, Linying
Zhang, Xiaojun
Qiao, Nan
Yu, Dayu
coredata.dc:description: Four kinds of bacterial cellulose (BC) were obtained by the stationary culture of Gluconacetobacter xylinus in molasses substitute medium, refined soybean oil wastewater, tofu product wastewater and barm washing effluent (BE) at 30 ℃ for 7 days, respectively. It was found that the BCs produced with different raw materials had differences in their porosity and compactness. The four kinds of BC were used to prepare carbon dots (CDs) using the hydrothermal method, respectively, confirming that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was positively correlated with the porosity of the BC. Compared to other sources of BC, the BC produced from BE had the highest porosity at 72.57 % and the loosest structure; thus, the resultant CDs (CDs-BE) displayed the strongest fluorescence intensity and were rich in hydroxyl groups. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to examine the effect of raw material micromorphology on the fluorescence properties of CDs. CDs-BE displayed excellent performance in detecting Fe3+ ions with a detection range of 10–250 µM and a detection limit of 74 nM. This study realized the resource utilization of wastewater by fermenting G. xylinus to produce BC, and then prepared CDs with excellent fluorescence properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacterial cellulose
Micromorphology
Carbon dots
Fluorescence property
Fe3+ ions detection
Food industry wastewaters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Food industry wastewater, culture media and microorganism
Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of metal ions by CDs-BE
Determination of Fe3+ in the actual water samples
Production of BC from food industry wastewaters
Yield and porosity of the BC
Detection of Fe3+ ions by CDs-BE
Establishment of the assay to detect Fe3+ ions
Analysis of Fe3+ ions in actual water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of CDs using BC as a carbon sources.
FT-IR spectra of the BCs from the different raw materials. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and a representative result is shown.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of four CDs; (B) optimal wavelength spectra of CDs-BE; (C) fluorescence spectra of CDs-BE at different excitation wavelengths; (D) fluorescence intensity of CDs-BE at different pH values. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and a representative result is shown (A, B and C). Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation for triplicate samples, and values with different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05) (D).
(A) TEM images; (B) FT-IR spectrum; (C) XRD image; (D) UV spectrum of CDs-BE. All experiments were performed in triplicate, and a representative result is shown.

(A) Metal ions selectivity of CDs-BE; (B) fluorescence intensity changes of CDs-BE over time; (C) fluorescence spectra of Fe3+ ions of different concentrations in CDs-BE solution; (D) standard curve of Fe3+ ion detection by CDs-BE. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation for triplicate samples (A and B), and values with different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05) (A). All experiments were performed in triplicate, and a representative result is shown (C and D).
The characteristics of the raw materials for producing BC.
Yield and porosity of the BC produced by fermentation in different raw materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation for triplicate samples, and values with different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of surface -OH and quantum yield of the four CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation for triplicate samples, and values with different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different CDs applied to detect Fe3+ ions.
Recoveries of Fe3+ in actual water samples detected by CDs-BE.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation for triplicate samples.

head.ce:title: Bacterial cellulose production from wastewater and the influence of its porosity on the fluorescence intensity of prepared carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Center of Clean Conversion and High-valued Utilization of Biomass, Northeast Electric Power University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Four kinds of bacterial cellulose (BC) were obtained by the stationary culture of Gluconacetobacter xylinus in molasses substitute medium, refined soybean oil wastewater, tofu product wastewater and barm washing effluent (BE) at 30 ℃ for 7 days, respectively. It was found that the BCs produced with different raw materials had differences in their porosity and compactness. The four kinds of BC were used to prepare carbon dots (CDs) using the hydrothermal method, respectively, confirming that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was positively correlated with the porosity of the BC. Compared to other sources of BC, the BC produced from BE had the highest porosity at 72.57 % and the loosest structure; thus, the resultant CDs (CDs-BE) displayed the strongest fluorescence intensity and were rich in hydroxyl groups. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to examine the effect of raw material micromorphology on the fluorescence properties of CDs. CDs-BE displayed excellent performance in detecting Fe3+ ions with a detection range of 10–250 µM and a detection limit of 74 nM. This study realized the resource utilization of wastewater by fermenting G. xylinus to produce BC, and then prepared CDs with excellent fluorescence properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The discharge of food industry wastewater has increased gradually with the rapid development of the food processing industry. Food industry wastewater belongs to high-concentration organic wastewater. For example, the wastewater from soybean oil production industries contains a large amount of oil [30]; the wastewater from tofu workshops contains sugars, amino acids, etc. [36]; the wastewater from breweries and wineries contain not only a large amount of carbohydrates but also a small amount of ethanol [15,55]. Therefore, the use of food industry wastewater to cultivate microorganisms and obtain high value-added products is an important aspect for the realization of wastewater resource utilization [11]. For example, nutrients in food industry wastewater can be used by bacteria, such as Gluconacetobacter xylinus to produce bacterial cellulose (BC), which is a type of polymer composed of glucose with high purity [2]. What is more noteworthy is that using these wastewaters to culture G. xylinus not only achieves the recycling of wastewater but also obtains BCs with different micromorphologies. For example, BC obtained from lipid fermentation wastewater has thick fibers and compact structure [13], but BC obtained from tofu liquid waste has a loose structure [1].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, only a few nanometers in size [21]. Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs, they have been widely used in cell imaging, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices and other fields [26,49]. Natural plants, synthetic organic matter and domestic wastes can all be used as raw materials for the synthesis of CDs [39]. Cellulose is an excellent raw material for the preparation of CDs, and cellulose-based CDs have been extensively reported. For example, using cellulose extracted from poplar powder, Song et al. [37] synthesized CDs with an average particle size of 3.5 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength of approximately 365 nm. Using these CDs, a composite material was further synthesized to adsorb Cr3+ in water, and the adsorption effect was monitored by the change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the material. [42] prepared water-soluble CDs with cellulose as a carbon source, and the CDs combined with isophorone diisocyanate and β-cyclodextrin showed a good detection effect on Cr3+ ions. As a polymer of glucose, BC like starch [47] and cellulose [37] can also be an ideal raw material for the preparation of CDs. Using BC to prepare CDs is method of high-value in applications of BC. It is worth noting that the BC obtained from different wastewaters have different micromorphologies. For example, using corn starch wastewater [5], distillery effluent [16] and lipid fermentation wastewater [13] as raw materials, respectively, the BCs with different structural looseness and pore sizes were produced in high yields. However, the influence of these differences in the micromorphologies of BC obtained from different sources on the obtained CDs had been ignored. Therefore, using BCs with different micromorphologies produced from different food industry wastewaters to prepare CDs is expected to reveal the relationship between the micromorphology of the raw materials and the fluorescence properties, and it will also widen the scope of of application for the utilization of resource products obtained from wastewater.

In this paper, molasses substitute medium (MM), refined soybean oil wastewater (SW), tofu product wastewater (TW), barm washing effluent (BE) and rich medium (RM) were used to produce BC-MM, BC-SW, BC-TW, BC-BE and BC-RM, respectively. Then, the BCs, as carbon sources, were used to prepare CDs-MM, CDs-SW, CDs-TW, CDs-BE and CDs-RM, respectively. Thereby, the effects of the different raw materials’ micromorphologies on the fluorescence properties of the CDs were studied, and the CDs with the best fluorescence properties were applied in the detection of metal ions, which improved the application value of the CDs prepared from BC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food industry wastewater, culture media and microorganism
ce:section.ce:para: The SW came from a soybean oil extraction plant, the BE came from a brewery and the TW came from a tofu workshop, all in Jilin City. All three types of wastewater were all centrifuged to remove ash, residue and other precipitates and then stored for later use. The MM was a molasses solution with a concentration of 60 g/L. Molasses is a by-product of sugar making processes, containing abundant sucrose and small amounts of amino acids, vitamins and trace elements. First, the molasses was diluted in water at a ratio of 1:0.3, and the pH was adjusted to 2.0–2.3 with sulfuric acid. After standing for 10 min, the colloid and ash were removed by centrifugation, and the solution was prepared by adding the water last. The major characteristics of the wastewaters and MM are shown in Table 1.

According to previous methods used by our research group, RM was used to preculture G. xylinus and also used as the control medium [30]. The preparation of RM was as follows: 0.5 g glucose, 0.5 g mannitol, 0.5 g polypeptone, 0.5 g yeast extract, 0.1 g magnesium sulfate and 100 mL deionized water were added into a 500 mL flask, which resulted in a 35,000 mg/L chemical oxygen demand (COD), and then the pH value was adjusted to between 6.6 and 7.0; furthermore, 0.5 mL ethanol was added after sterilization to promote the growth of G. xylinus. One hundred milliliters of MM, SW, TW and BE were added to a series of 500 mL flasks, respectively. The pH value of the wastewaters and MM was adjusted to between 6.6 and 7.0 and sterilized at 121 ℃ for 15 min
All the reagents used in the experiments were of an analytical or biotechnological grade. The G. xylinus used in this study was obtained from laboratory-preserved strains.

The preculture of G. xylinus was performed in RM at 30 ℃ and 150 rpm for 24 h. Then, 10 % seed liquid of G. xylinus was inoculated into the wastewaters and MM, and the fermentation took place at 30 ℃ under static conditions.

For the purification of the BC, we referred to a previous study by our group [30]: First, the surface of BC membrane was rinsed 2–3 times with deionized water to remove the culture medium and impurities on the surface. Then, the BC membrane was immersed in a 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution at 80 ℃ to remove the cells and the residual medium until it became translucent. Next, the membrane was transferred to a 0.1 % acetic acid solution for 2 h and then rinsed with deionized water to become neutral. Finally, the BC membrane was dried to a constant weight in a blast drying oven [18].
The yield (g/g (COD added)) of the BC was the ratio of the dry weight of the BC produced from the wastewater to the COD in the same volume of wastewater.

The porosity (%) of the BC was calculated using the medium saturation method [28]. The BC with a dry weight of W0 was immersed in deionized water to fully absorb the water. After taking it out, the film was gently wiped with filter paper, absorbing the water, and then it was put on an analytical balance, and the wet weight was weighed as W1. The weight of the water absorbed by the sample was W1-W0, then the pore volume of the membrane was W1-W0/ρ, and ρ was the density of water. A beaker filled with water was weighed as W2. The wet film was put into the beaker and then filled with deionized water and weighed as W3; then, the weight of the water discharged by the wet film was W2- (W3-W1), the apparent volume of the BC was W2- (W3-W1) /ρ and ρ was the density of the water. To calculate the porosity (ε), we used the following formula:
ε =
W
1
−
W
0
W
2
− (
W
3
−
W
1
)
× 100 %

Characterization of the BC: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Philips, XL-30 ESEM) was used to observe the micromorphology of the BC at 10 kV acceleration voltage. Fourier infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Shimadzu, IRAffinity-1) was used to analyze the groups of the BC in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The diffractogram was recorded at room temperature using Ni-filtered Cu Kα X-ray radiation. Under the condition of working voltage of 40 kV and current of 40 mA, the BC was scanned from 5° to 70° with a scan speed of 2°/min. The pattern from 5° to 55° was shown.

Cellulose is one of the main raw materials used in the preparation of sugar-based CDs, and it can be degraded to produce CDs in high-temperature hydrothermal environments (>140 ℃) [24]. Using four kinds of BC, respectively, CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method. First, the dried BC was cut into small pieces approximately 1 mm in size. Then, 0.5 g of the BC and deionized water (30 mL) were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and the mixture was stirred with a magnetic agitator for 5 min. This was followed by a 5 h hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃. After cooling to room temperature, the dispersed carbon solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The resultant supernatant liquid was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter, and then finally dialyzed for 2–3 days using a dialysis bag with an aperture of 1000 Da [49]. The preparation process for the CDs is shown in Fig. 1.

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL) (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC) with a scan speed of super and a slit ratio of 5:5. The size distribution and morphology of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) with an operating voltage of 200 kV. The functional groups of the CDs were analyzed by FT-IR. A small amount of sample powder and potassium bromide were ground and pressed into sheets and then put into the test tank for measurement with a scanning range of 4000–400 cm−1. The ultraviolet absorption spectra of the CDs were measured by an ultraviolet spectrometer (UV) (Shimadzu, UV-2550) with a scanning speed of medium and a scanning range of 200–800 nm. The crystal structure of the CDs was analyzed with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Shimadzu, XRD-7000). The diffractogram was recorded at room temperature using Ni-filtered Cu Kα X-ray radiation. The sample was scanned from 10° to 70° with a scan speed of 2°/min, and the operating voltage and current were 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively.

For the determination of the number of hydroxyl groups (-OH) on the surface of the CDs (Ouyang et al., 2005), firstly, a 120 mL solution of CDs was freeze-dried into a solid powder and placed in a 200 mL beaker. Then, 25 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 75 mL of 20 % NaCl solution were put into the beaker. Next, 1 mol/L of HCl and 0.1 mol/L of NaOH were used to adjust the pH of the solution to 4. Finally, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 9 and remained stable by slowly adding 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution. To calculate the number of -OH, we used the following formula:
N=CVNA×10−3
where N is the number of -OH; C is the concentration of NaOH, 0.1 mol/L; V is the volume (mL) of 0.1 mol/L NaOH consumed when the pH rises from 4.0 to 9.0; NA is Avogadro's number, which equals 6.022 × 1023.

The relative contents of the different oxygen-containing functional groups of the CDs were analyzed semi-quantitatively by an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and calculated using Vision software and the Shirley baseline. Finally, XPS Peak software was used for data processing and fitting. The relative contents of C-C, C-O and CO were obtained by integration, and the relative content of -OH was the difference between the relative contents of C-O and CO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum yield refers to the utilization of light quanta in photochemical reactions. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as the standard, and the quantum yield at 360 nm was approximately 0.54. The absorbance and fluorescence emission spectra of the standard substance and CDs at 360 nm were measured. The peak area of all of the fluorescence emission spectra measured above was obtained using Origin software. The fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs was calculated according to the following formula [40]:
φX=φS(FX/FS)(AS/AX)(ηX/ηR)2
where R and X represents the reference and CDs, respectively; the parameter φ refers to the quantum yield; A is the absorbance value; F is the integrated fluorescence intensity; η denotes the refractive index of the solvent medium (the refractive index η of water = 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions by CDs-BE
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity test of the metal cations: First, 11 different metal ions (i.e., Fe3+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Li+, Mn2+ and Cu2+) were selected. Next, 1 mL of 0.4 mM metal ion solution and 1 mL of CDs-BE were added to a quartz cuvette, and mixed evenly. After standing for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded.

Determination of the detection response time: 1 mL of 0.4 mM Fe3+ ion solution and 1 mL of CDs-BE were added to a quartz cuvette, and mixed evenly. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured by FL, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded by scanning every 30 s

Establishment of a standard curve: Fe3+ ion solutions with different concentrations (i.e., 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2 and 0.25 mM) were prepared. Then, 1 mL of Fe3+ ion solution and 1 mL of CDs-BE were mixed evenly in quartz colorimetric dishes. After standing for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were measured, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded. The standard curve was established using the fluorescence intensity as the ordinate and the Fe3+ ions concentration as the abscissa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in the actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The purified tap water came from household drinking water, and the industrial circulating cooling water came from a heating company in Jilin City. First, the above two actual water samples were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. Then, 1 mL of the actual water sample and 1 mL of CDs-BE were added to a quartz cuvette and mixed evenly. After standing for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded. As a control, the concentration of Fe3+ was determined by EDTA titration [51] for the two actual water samples.

All of the above experiments were tested in three parallel samples. The data were analyzed using the Excel and SPSS and are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation of the three samples. The data were tested for normal distribution and variance heterogeneity using Q test, and the Tukey multiple comparisons in one-way ANOVA were used for statistical difference analysis. All analyses were considered statistically significant when the P value was less than 0.05. Origin software was used to plot the data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of BC from food industry wastewaters
Yield and porosity of the BC
ce:section.ce:para: The yield and porosity of the BCs produced by fermenting G. xylinus in different raw materials are shown in Table 2. It can be observed that the yields of BC in the four types of food-industry-related raw materials were not lower than that in the control, and the porosities of the BCs were different. The BC produced from BE displayed the highest porosity of 72.57 %. Two reasons may have contributed to this outcome: One is that the carbohydrates in BE are more readily available and metabolizable by G. xylinus than the oils in the SW, which has low solubility in water and requires a longer metabolic pathway to be converted to cellulose [30]. The second is the small amount of ethanol contained in the BE, which can promote the growth of G. xylinus [10]. In addition, the yields of BC in this study are different from those reported in other studies. For example, the yields of BC obtained by Tang et al. [38] using glucose, fructose, sucrose and glycerol as carbon sources were 5.54, 5.26, 2.72 and 6.20 g/L, respectively. Lin et al. [22] used waste beer yeast hydrolysates with a sugar concentration of 3% to produce BC, and the yield of BC per liter of wastewater was 7.02 g. This may be attributed to the differences in the bacterial strain, medium composition or inoculation amount [10,15].

The micromorphology of the BC was observed using SEM. The A, B, C and D of Fig. S1 (in Supplementary Information) show SEM images of the BCs produced by fermentation in MM, SW, TW and BE, respectively. It can be clearly observed that the microfibers of the BC interweaved with each other to form a network of nanofibers. By measuring the sizes of the four BCs’ fibers, as shown in Fig. S1, it was obtained that the sizes of the four BCs’ fibers were all in the range of 20–40 nm. The main nanofiber structure of the BC produced from the four raw materials was similar to the results of SEM analyses reported in other studies [52]. However, it is worth noting that the pore size and compactness of the four BC fiber networks were different. The BC produced using SW had the smallest pore size and the densest structure, while that produced using BE had the largest pore size and the loosest structure. As previously reported, the BC obtained from Tsingtao beer yeast residue also had a large pore size and a loose structure, and the pores of the fibers were filled with water molecules; thus, the BC had a high water-holding capacity [22]. A similar morphology was observed for BC produced from the hydrolysate of agricultural corn stalks [3]. However, using liquid tapioca waste, the nanofibers of the BC formed a thin, dense network [27]. G. xylinus displayed differences in the utilization of the nutrients in the different wastewaters [30], and the fermentation inhibitory substances in the wastewater may affect the synthesis of the BC [3], which would lead to differences in the structural porosity and looseness of the BCs.

The functional groups on the BC polymers were analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2, the characteristic absorption bands of the FT-IR spectra mainly included bands at 3350, 2920, 1650, 1430, 1650, 1160, 1035 and 896 cm−1. The broad absorption bands at approximately 3350 cm−1 were related to the presence of -OH, indicating that the BCs contained a large number of -OH. The absorption bands near 2920 and 1650 cm−1 were associated with the stretching of C-H and the presence of CO. The absorption bands at 1430, 1160, 1035 and 896 cm−1 were characteristic bands of cellulose I [30]. Among them, the typical absorption bands at 896 cm−1 were attributed to β-linked glucose polymers, indicating that the BC was composed of glucose. By comparing the FT-IR spectra of the BCs produced in the different raw materials, it was found that there were similar absorption bands in these spectra, indicating that the BCs produced by fermentation in different food industry wastewaters and MM had similar chemical structures. Our research group previously analyzed the element and sugar composition of BC, and found that BC was composed of C, H and O, and glucose was its only monosaccharide composition [30]. In summary, the BCs obtained in this study had the same chemical structure with differences only in their micromorphologies, which provided the condition for analyzing the relationship between the micromorphologies of the raw materials and the fluorescence properties of the CDs.

It can clearly be observed in Fig. S2 (in Supplementary Information) that the XRD patterns of the five types of BC had obvious peaks at 2θ angles of 14.6°, 16.7° and 22.7°, which corresponded to the 100, 010 and 110 crystal planes, respectively. Compared with the ideal XRD pattern of cellulose Iα, the peak corresponding to the 010 crystal plane was nearly absent, which may be because BC is cellulose Iα with a triclinic cell and has additional orientation around the fibers’ axis [9]. The BCs produced by other raw materials, such as bagasse hydrolysates [29], makgeolli sludge filtrate and Hestrin–Schramm medium [15], had similar XRD patterns. BC is a highly pure glucose polymer, and the fibrous network of BC may contain residual bacteria and medium components in the production process [14]. However, in the purification of BC, the residues were almost completely removed. The XRD patterns showed that the BCs obtained from different raw materials were all cellulose Ⅰα, and the absence of other peaks indicated the high purity of the BCs. Additionally, the different peak intensities indicated that the crystallization of the five kinds of BC was different, and a smaller peak intensity indicated that the sample had more amorphous regions [29]. Based on the data obtained by XPS, the crystallinity indices of the BC-RM, BC-MM, BC-SW, BC-TW and BC-BE calculated by Origin were 74.45%, 53.16%, 63.81%, 42.74%, and 38.09%, respectively. The crystallinity index of BC-BE was lower than that of the other four kinds of BC, which may be due to the looser structure and larger porosity of BC-BE. In other studies, it was found that BCs with a tightly meshed fiber structures had high crystallinity, while BCs with low crystallinity had a high water holding capacity [44]. As shown in Fig. S2, the peaks of the BC obtained from BE were lower compared to those from the other raw materials, which may correspond to the looser microstructure of the BC.

Fluorescence is the most basic and significant property of CDs. The results of the fluorescence spectra showed that the order of fluorescence intensity from strong to weak was CDs-BE, CDs-TW, CDs-MM and CDs-SW under the same hydrothermal conditions ( Fig. 3A). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the above CDs was positively correlated with the porosity of the BC. The reason for this may be that the BCs with a larger porosity and looser structure had a higher water holding capacity [22], and they fully contacted with the water to allow for more cellulose to participate in the hydrothermal reaction; thus, the CDs with a stronger fluorescence were obtained. Differences in the compositions of the raw materials would cause differences in the properties of the CDs [54]. However, in this study, the four BCs with the same compositions only had differences in the porosity and structural looseness, and the four kinds of CDs were prepared under the same conditions. Therefore, it was inferred that difference in the micromorphologies of the BCs could cause differences in the fluorescence properties of the CDs. At present, researchers are focused on the effects of differences in the raw materials and the preparation methods on the fluorescence performance of CDs [6,23]. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, it was found that CDs prepared from BCs with a higher porosity and looser structure had a higher fluorescence intensity. This finding is beneficial for the controlled preparation of BC-based CDs with high fluorescence. In addition, the preparation of the CDs only took 0.5 g BC, which was one-twentieth of what Li et al. used, and did not consume other ingredients, such as citric acid [19,20], which is conducive to studying the properties of the CDs prepared from BC. At the same time, BC from wastewater was used to prepare CDs, which provided an idea for expanding the application of wastewater-derived products.

Then, CDs-BE, which had the best fluorescence intensity in this study, was selected for a series of characteristic analyses. As shown in Fig. 3B, the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs-BE was 360 nm, and the emission wavelength was 449 nm in the blue region of the visible spectrum. Therefore, 360 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the CDs-BE was calculated to be 26% using quinine sulfate as a reference, and the quantum yields of the other three CDs were not significantly different from those of CDs-BE as shown in Table 3. Compared with CDs prepared from other materials, such as microcrystalline cellulose [45] and pine wood [53], the quantum yields of the CDs prepared in this paper were higher. As shown in Fig. 3C, with the increase in the excitation wavelength from 320 to 420 nm, the emission peak of CDs-BE redshifted; meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased, and the strongest emission spectrum was obtained at 360 nm. The CDs also showed a similar phenomenon in other studies. For example, the CDs prepared by Khan et al. [17] showed a redshift of the emission peak with an increase in the excitation wavelength from 280 to 420 nm. This phenomenon is consistent with excitation wavelength dependence, which is an unique feature of CDs [32]. There are two possible reasons for the redshift of the emission peak: one is that CD nanoparticles of different sizes had differences in their selectivity to light, and the other was related to surface defects of CDs [56].

In order to exam the fluorescence stability, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BE under various pH values was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BE was stable over a wide pH range, and it decreased only a little under strong acidic and alkaline conditions. The decrease may be because the groups on the surface of the CDs protonated and deprotonated under the extreme conditions, resulting in a change in surface charge [7]. The results of this study show that CDs-BE had good pH stability. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs prepared by [19,20] gradually increased with the increase in the value of pH, in the range of 3–7, and when the pH value was higher than 7, the fluorescence intensity remained stable. When the pH value was higher than 8.5, the fluorescence of the CDs prepared by [42] quenched observably. Thus, compared with these CDs, CDs-BE are more suitable for neutral conditions.

As shown in Fig. 4A, CDs-BE are a kind of spherical nanoparticle with good dispersion, and the average particle size was approximately 4.14 nm similar to the previously reported cellulose-based CDs [37,42,43]. As shown in the upper right corner of Fig. 4A, CDs-BE had lattice fringes with a crystal spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating that they had a graphite structure [8].

As shown in Fig. 4B, the characteristic absorption bands of the FT-IR spectrum mainly included bands at 3350, 2930, 1650, 1390, 1244 and 1050 cm−1. The absorption band near 3350 cm−1 with a relatively wide range corresponded to -OH, which may be related to the large amount of -OH contained in BC. The absorption bands at 2930 and 1390 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration and symmetric bending of CH2, respectively [12]. The weak absorption band at 1650 cm−1 was associated with the presence of CO [33]. The absorption bands at 1050 and 1244 cm−1 were attributed to alcoholic hydroxyl and phenolic hydroxyl, respectively, or ether groups (C-O-C) [17,42,43]. The absorption bands at 650–900 cm−1 were related to the presence of the benzene ring. Overall, it was indicated that there were a large number of -OH and carboxy groups (-COOH) on the surface of CDs-BE. The presence of -OH and -COOH provides CDs-BE with good water solubility and stability, indicating that CDs-BE have great application potential in water-transfer sensing [50]. Zhao et al. [53] used pine as a carbon source to prepare CDs that contained a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups such as -OH and -COOH, and the functional groups could interact with Fe3+ ions to quantitatively detect the ions. Therefore, it was speculated that CDs-BE could also be used to detect Fe3+ ions.

The XRD image (Fig. 4 C) showed a wide peak at a 2θ angle of 20°, which corresponded to the 002 crystal plane of carbon, indicating that CDs-BE had an amorphous carbon structure [17]. The optical absorption of the CDs mainly occurred in the ultraviolet region [35]. The UV spectrum (Fig. 4D) showed that CDs-BE had an absorption band at 278 nm, which was attributed to the π–π * transition of the CC bond [41]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4D, the CDs-BE solution was light brown under the irradiation of white light and emitted blue light under the irradiation of ultraviolet light at 365 nm.

In previous studies, BC with a looser micromorphology was found to have a lower crystallinity, and the lower the crystallinity of the BC, the weaker the hydrogen bonds between molecules (Cao et al., 2011). Thus, in the hydrothermal reaction process, the -OH in the BC with a looser micromorphology is more easily involved in the reaction. As a result, the amount of -OH on the surface of the CDs may be different. In addition, -OH has a certain ability to supply electrons, which is more conducive to carbon point fluorescence emission (Liu, 2011). As shown in the above FT-IR analysis, there was a large number of -OH on the surface of CDs-BE, which had the best fluorescence intensity in this study. The differences in the fluorescence intensity of the four CDs may be attributed to the amount of -OH on the surface of the CDs. Therefore, it was necessary to analyze the amount of -OH on the surface of the four CDs prepared using BC with different micromorphologies.

The number of -OH on the surface of the four CDs is shown in Table 3. The -OH content on the surface of the CDs was positively correlated with its fluorescence intensity. Among the four CDs, 120 mL of CDs-BE with the highest fluorescence intensity contained the most -OH. The functional groups and elemental states of the CDs were further analyzed by XPS. The two peaks shown in Fig. S3A (in Supplementary Information) represent the elements carbon and oxygen in the CDs. Fig. S3B, C, D and E display the curve-fitted C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of the four CDs. The peak centered at 284.8 eV could be deconvoluted into three contributions at 284.5, 285.7 and 287.6 eV, which were associated with carbon in the states of C-C, C-O and CO, respectively. Further quantitative analysis indicated that the percentages of C-O, CO and -OH on the four CDs were different (Table S1 (in Supplementary Information)). CDs-BE had the largest percentage of -OH on the surface; thus, its fluorescence intensity was the highest.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions by CDs-BE
Establishment of the assay to detect Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Based on its good fluorescence properties and abundant functional groups [31,34,35], CDs-BE was directly applied to the detection of the different metal ions at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. It can be clearly observed from Fig. 5A that only Fe3+ ions could effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs-BE solution, while the change in the fluorescence intensity of the solution after the addition of other metal ions was negligible. Thus, it was proved that CDs-BE had a single selectivity to Fe3+ ions. The reaction mechanism between CDs-BE and Fe3+ ions was similar to that previously reported [4]. When Fe3+ ions were added to the CD solution, the ions could interact with the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the CDs to form complexes, resulting in higher energy electrons transfer from the surface of the CDs to the 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ions; therefore, the surface defects of the CDs increased and nonradiative transition was promoted. Thus, a significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed in the CDs-BE solution containing Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5B, when Fe3+ ions were added to the CDs-BE solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly and reached a stable state after 2 min, indicating that the quenching process was sensitive and rapid. A standard curve was established by testing the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BE solution after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BE maintained a good linear relationship with a series of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions in the range of 10–250 µM (Fig. 5 C). With the increase in the Fe3+ ion concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the solution decreased continuously, with a detection limit (LOD; at a S/N ratio of 3) of approximately 74 nM, and the linear equation obtained was Y= 430.16184–1.23121X, with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9973 (Fig. 5D). The LOD of CDs-BE for Fe3+ ions in this study was comparable to or even better than other reported results ( Table 4). In conclusion, due to the fact of their high sensitivity, high selectivity and fast response, CDs-BE is a highly effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ ions in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of this method in actual water samples, this approach was employed to detect Fe3+ ions in purified tap water and industrial circulating cooling water. It is notable that the recoveries were close to 100%, as listed in Table 5. Therefore, the fluorescence detection method had a good detection effect and can be used for the analysis of actual water samples.

In this paper, BC was produced by fermentation in different food-industry-related raw materials and then used as carbon sources to prepare CDs-MM, CDs-SW, CDs-TW and CDs-BE. It was confirmed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was positively correlated with the porosity of the BC. In these CDs, CDs-BE prepared using BC with the loosest structure and the highest porosity had the strongest fluorescence intensity and the most -OH. There were many oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs-BE. Therefore, CDs-BE showed a good detection effect on Fe3+ ions. In the range of 10–250 µM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-BE and the concentration of Fe3+ ions showed a good linear relationship, and the LOD was 74 nM.

Huanyu Zhou: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jing Wang: Investigation. Heng Yin: Investigation. Chuandong Wang: Investigation. Linying Zhang: Methodology. Xiaojun Zhang: Data curation. Nan Qiao: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Dayu Yu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (20220101083JC, 20190902014TC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bej.2022.108633.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
